result: Auroras fascinate me because they demonstrate, in shimmering curtains of emerald and violet, a conversation between our planet and the sun. Particles racing from solar flares plunge into Earth’s magnetic field, funneling down to polar latitudes, where atoms of oxygen and nitrogen awaken into light. I imagine communities bundled in parkas, craning their necks, silently cheerleading as science paints the sky. A few years ago I watched an aurora in Alaska. The night felt like a cold sheet wrapped around my shoulders, but the colors kept me rooted. They rippled like whales exhaling under moonlit water. I wanted to bottle the hum of that night and unscrew the lid whenever city neon felt substitute bright. Back home, I painted my living room ceiling with phosphorescent pigments. Under daylight the ceiling appears ordinary white; at night, after charging lamps, it glows softly, a homemade aurora. Friends think it’s quirky, but I love drifting off beneath my private lights. They remind me that wonder isn’t limited to remote destinations. Wonder can be curated, coaxed, and crafted inside small apartments, through books, through whispered stories, through ritual cups of tea. I keep a notebook labeled “Daily Northern Lights” where I record any moment that tingles the spine—an overheard compliment between strangers, a perfect avocado, a poem that invents new verbs. The notebook fills slowly because not every day is awe-struck. Yet pausing to notice, to make a ledger entry for wonder, feels like tuning a radio to a frequency that’s always there.
